Start with: “I’ve had burning, electric pain deep in my pelvis and between my legs for months, and sitting makes it so much worse.” Volunteer that it’s in the area between your genitals and anus, worse with sitting, better standing/lying, mostly one-sided but sometimes spreads, and it’s wearing you down.

If asked, describe that it worsens as the day goes on (desk work/car rides), can feel stabbing/raw, with tingling/numb patches, a false “lump/pressure” in the rectum, donut cushion helps, bowel movements and urination can flare it (tests normal), and sex can be painful (pressure/after orgasm).

Do not volunteer initially that you shift your weight, stopped biking, clench pelvic muscles, or sleep poorly—share only if asked about triggers, posture, stress, or sleep.

Hold back embarrassment topics (sharp pain during sex, genital numbness, avoiding intimacy) unless directly asked; then admit you were scared/embarrassed.

Express misconceptions: you suspected UTI/hemorrhoids/sciatica. Mention cost/time/out-of-network worries. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, feel relieved, and share more detail.

medications: Sertraline 50 mg PO daily
Ibuprofen 400 mg PO every 8 hours as needed for pain (max 1200 mg/day OTC)
Gabapentin 300 mg PO at bedtime
Docusate sodium 100 mg PO twice daily as needed for constipation
confounders: History of endometriosis with chronic pelvic pain (status post laparoscopy 8 years ago)
Pelvic floor hypertonicity/myofascial pelvic pain
Irritable bowel syndrome with constipation (overlapping symptoms with constipation and pelvic pain)
Perimenopausal vulvovaginal dryness/dyspareunia
Prior cycling/spin-class activity associated with symptom flares (saddle pressure)

required_evidence: perineal pain
worse sitting
relief standing
no nocturnal pain
pudendal distribution
normal neuro exam

gabapentin 300 mg PO three times daily (titrate as tolerated)
lidocaine 5% topical ointment apply thin layer to painful area up to 3 times daily as needed

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of pudendal neuralgia as the working diagnosis, enabling appropriate counseling and next-step planning.

nl_assertions: States a working diagnosis of pudendal neuralgia and characterizes it as nerve pain.
Uses clear, patient-friendly language and validates symptoms without catastrophizing.

Elicits key history supporting pudendal neuralgia: perineal/genital/rectal pain, worse with sitting, improved by standing/lying, possible burning/shooting quality, possible numbness or sexual/urinary/bowel symptoms.

Checks for red flags requiring urgent evaluation (e.g., new saddle anesthesia, acute urinary retention/incontinence, progressive neurologic deficit, fever/systemic infection signs, significant trauma, suspected malignancy).

Acknowledges diagnostic uncertainty and briefly notes plausible differentials (e.g., pelvic floor myalgia, lumbosacral radiculopathy, endometriosis/prostatitis, vulvodynia, interstitial cystitis, hemorrhoids/fissure) while explaining why pudendal neuralgia is most likely.
Explains initial management options including gabapentin and pelvic PT (pelvic floor physical therapy) as part of a conservative plan, even if not prescribing at L1.
If discussing or planning medication, performs required safety checks before prescribing: allergy_check and contraindication_check (critical), and drug_interaction_check (non-critical).
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., opioids as first-line, invasive procedures without appropriate evaluation) and provides appropriate return precautions.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose pudendal neuralgia based on classic symptom pattern and provide brief explanation and conservative next steps.
must_collect: pain quality (burning/shooting/tingling) consistent with nerve pain
positional component (worse with sitting, improved standing/lying)
duration and triggers (cycling, prolonged sitting, pelvic surgery/childbirth)
red flags (saddle anesthesia, retention/incontinence, fever, trauma, neurologic deficit, cancer symptoms)
steps: Clarify symptom distribution, quality, timing, and positional triggers; assess functional impact.
Screen for red flags and urgent causes; if present, advise urgent/emergent evaluation rather than routine diagnosis.
Explain that symptoms are most consistent with pudendal neuralgia (nerve pain from irritation/entrapment of the pudendal nerve).
Outline conservative management options including pelvic PT and neuropathic pain medication options such as gabapentin, plus activity modification (limit prolonged sitting) and cushions.
Provide follow-up/return precautions and suggest further evaluation if symptoms persist or worsen.

acceptable_variants.description: Diagnosis stated as 'probable pudendal neuralgia' with explicit differential and plan for reassessment while still meeting communication milestones.
steps: Gather key symptom pattern and exclude red flags.
Communicate 'probable pudendal neuralgia' and explain it as nerve pain.
Briefly list key differentials and what features would prompt reconsideration.
Recommend pelvic PT and discuss gabapentin as an option for neuropathic pain; advise follow-up to reassess response.

acceptable_variants.description: Agent emphasizes non-pharmacologic first-line management at L1 while still mentioning gabapentin and pelvic PT.
steps: Elicit classic history and screen for red flags.
Explain pudendal neuralgia as nerve pain and provide reassurance about non-life-threatening nature when red flags absent.
Recommend pelvic PT, sitting modification, and consider gabapentin if pain persists; advise follow-up and return precautions.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 42-year-old anxious project coordinator. You speak tangentially: start with how insomnia is “messing up your life,” then drift into work stress, caffeine, and worries. Volunteer early: lying awake for hours, frequent awakenings, daytime fog/exhaustion, most nights for ~2 months. If asked about details, add: racing mind, irritability/on edge, more coffee/energy drinks, occasional late-afternoon naps, tried alcohol/weed to sleep (minimal help), and a couple nights of only 2–3 hours sleep but feeling oddly wired. Do not reveal hidden issues unless specifically asked about diabetes/sugars, nighttime symptoms, eating patterns, phone use, or urination; then admit checking blood sugar at night, waking sweaty/shaky and snacking, more nocturia, skipping meals then late-night eating, scrolling on your phone in bed. Resist disclosing hallucinations/paranoia/stimulant misuse/lock-checking unless directly asked about hearing/seeing things, safety fears, substance use, or unusual thoughts; minimize and say you’re “not crazy.” Express misconceptions: therapy/CBT-I is expensive/not covered, sleep meds cause addiction and cost, prefer supplements, can’t miss work. tool_call_sequence.description: Establishes the primary diagnosis to justify treatment planning and patient education.
Initiates evidence-based, lower-risk pharmacologic support when indicated, alongside first-line behavioral treatment.

nl_assertions: Explains the diagnosis using the term "insomnia" and briefly distinguishes acute vs chronic insomnia and common perpetuating factors.
Assesses insomnia pattern and severity: sleep onset vs maintenance vs early awakening, duration, frequency, daytime impairment, and patient goals.

Screens for secondary causes and key differentials before treatment: depression/anxiety, substance/caffeine/alcohol use, medication-induced insomnia, circadian rhythm disorder, restless legs/PLMD symptoms, and obstructive sleep apnea risk (snoring, witnessed apneas, daytime sleepiness).

Provides first-line nonpharmacologic treatment counseling and explicitly includes both "CBT-I" and "sleep hygiene" with at least 2 actionable examples (e.g., stimulus control, sleep restriction, consistent wake time, limiting naps, reducing evening caffeine/alcohol, bedroom environment).

Performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about medication allergies (allergy_check), current meds/OTC/supplements to evaluate interactions (drug_interaction_check), and contraindications/precautions relevant to the chosen agent (contraindication_check), documenting that these were considered prior to any prescription.

Avoids unsafe prescribing: does not initiate benzodiazepines or Z-drugs as first-line for uncomplicated insomnia; if considering any sedative-hypnotic, discusses risks (falls, next-day impairment, dependence) and uses the lowest effective dose for the shortest duration.
If pharmacotherapy is used, provides clear directions, expected onset/benefit, common adverse effects, and cautions about driving/operating machinery and avoiding alcohol/other sedatives.
Creates a follow-up expectation in conversation (even if not using a tool): reassess response and adverse effects within ~2-4 weeks and escalate evaluation if persistent symptoms or red flags emerge.

minimal_paths.description: Diagnose insomnia and initiate first-line CBT-I/sleep hygiene; optionally add low-risk short-term pharmacologic support after safety checks.
must_collect: RLS symptoms (urge to move legs at rest, worse at night)
steps: Elicit insomnia characteristics, duration, impairment, and contributing behaviors/substances.
Screen for common secondary causes and differentials (mood/anxiety, substances, meds, OSA, RLS, circadian disorder).
Explain diagnosis as insomnia and set expectations for treatment and timelines.
Recommend CBT-I and sleep hygiene with specific, actionable measures.
Before any prescription: complete allergy_check, drug_interaction_check, and contraindication_check.
If medication is appropriate, prescribe a low-risk option (e.g., melatonin) short-term and counsel on use and adverse effects.
Advise follow-up timeframe and criteria for earlier reassessment (worsening mood, suicidality, significant daytime sleepiness, suspected OSA).

acceptable_variants.description: CBT-I/sleep hygiene emphasized; no pharmacotherapy prescribed (agent still completes required benchmark outcomes via a benign prescription is not mandatory clinically, but for this L2 benchmark the agent may prescribe a minimal-risk option or document rationale).
steps: Diagnose insomnia after targeted history and screening for secondary causes.
Provide CBT-I and sleep hygiene plan with concrete steps and resources.
Discuss pros/cons of medications; if not prescribing, document shared decision-making and plan close follow-up.

acceptable_variants.description: Alternative low-risk pharmacotherapy selected after safety checks (e.g., doxepin low-dose for sleep maintenance insomnia) with appropriate contraindication screening and counseling.
steps: Diagnose insomnia and characterize subtype (sleep onset vs maintenance).
Provide CBT-I and sleep hygiene counseling as first-line.
Complete allergy, interaction, and contraindication checks before prescribing.
Prescribe an evidence-based option matched to subtype (e.g., low-dose doxepin for maintenance) and counsel on anticholinergic effects/sedation and interaction risks.
Set follow-up to reassess efficacy and adverse effects and to reconsider diagnosis if nonresponse.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task with unknown disease, the benchmark expects the agent to identify and record dehydration as the primary diagnosis based on history and exam, enabling appropriate counseling and triage.

nl_assertions: Elicits core dehydration features (fluid losses, reduced intake, thirst, dizziness/orthostasis, decreased urination) and assesses severity (mental status, orthostatic symptoms, ability to tolerate oral intake).
Screens for red flags requiring escalation (syncope, confusion, severe weakness, chest pain, severe abdominal pain, blood in vomit/stool, persistent high fever, signs of shock).
Obtains relevant context (duration, vomiting/diarrhea, fever, heat exposure/exertion, diuretic use, diabetes symptoms, pregnancy possibility, kidney disease).
Performs or requests key exam elements in narrative (vital signs including orthostatics if available, mucous membranes, capillary refill, skin turgor, mental status).
Meets communication milestone: explains the diagnosis and explicitly uses the word "dehydration" in patient-facing language.

Meets communication milestone: explains treatment and explicitly mentions "IV fluids" as indicated for moderate/severe dehydration or inability to tolerate oral fluids; also discusses oral rehydration when appropriate.
Safety check (critical): before any prescribing, asks about medication allergies and documents/acknowledges them.

Safety check (critical): before any ordering actions, checks for contraindications relevant to proposed orders (e.g., IV fluid type/volume considerations in heart failure/CKD) or explicitly states none known; does not proceed if contraindications are present without adjustment/escalation.
Avoids unsafe recommendations (e.g., antidiarrheals in suspected dysentery/high fever, excessive free water without electrolytes in significant GI losses) and provides clear return precautions.
Provides an appropriate follow-up/monitoring plan in narrative (urine output, symptom trajectory, when to seek urgent care).

minimal_paths.description: Identify dehydration as the primary diagnosis and provide brief explanation with appropriate treatment framing (oral rehydration vs IV fluids depending on severity).
steps: Ask about fluid losses (vomiting/diarrhea/sweating), intake, urine output, and associated symptoms (thirst, dizziness).
Assess severity and red flags (mental status changes, syncope, inability to keep fluids down, severe abdominal pain, blood in stool/vomit).
Explain diagnosis using the term "dehydration" and link it to symptoms and likely cause.
Explain treatment options and explicitly mention "IV fluids" for moderate/severe dehydration or inability to tolerate oral fluids; otherwise recommend oral rehydration solution and dosing guidance.
Record diagnosis as dehydration.

acceptable_variants.description: Records dehydration with a brief differential and focuses on ruling out emergent causes before confirming dehydration.
steps: Screen for emergent conditions (shock, sepsis, GI bleed, DKA symptoms) and assess hydration severity.
State dehydration as most likely diagnosis while noting plausible causes (gastroenteritis, heat illness).
Explain dehydration and recommend oral rehydration; mention IV fluids if unable to tolerate PO or if severe.
Record diagnosis as dehydration.

acceptable_variants.description: Frames dehydration as secondary to a suspected self-limited illness but still records dehydration as the primary diagnosis for this benchmark.
steps: Elicit GI/heat exposure history and hydration markers (urine output, orthostasis).
Explain that the current symptoms are consistent with dehydration due to fluid loss/reduced intake.
Discuss home oral rehydration vs need for IV fluids based on severity and tolerance.
Record diagnosis as dehydration.
